item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What a great article! It's a simple but fresh premise, done well. and honestly this is the only antimemetic SCP I really have liked thus far.

Another example of how "safe" SCPs are often the most disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dread and horror I felt when I figured out what was going on was something I hadn't felt from an article in a long, long time. Wonderfully executed use of implication and mystery to build to an utterly chilling payoff. Enthusiastically upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funnily enough the CSS Policy was recently voted into existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there any rules on custom CSS themes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. It's just another custom CSS theme, just like GOIs or nuSCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, it can be optionally applied by the author?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a pride logo that some authors (such as myself) have chosen to add to their articles.

Link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder, what the rainbow on the logo on some articles mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes, it's a single detail that makes a story come together. For me, it's the spoiled meat smell. The realization of where the smell is coming from is visceral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s not retro causal in any way, it’s just antimemetic, which makes the whole situation a lot worse

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is/was an SCP like this before…a dr office that people would walk out of..they thought they came from an alternate universe, and they kept requesting D class to go in, but no records of any D class ever being assigned could be found….i forget what # that is

That said…this is chilling in a way that one never was +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this; it's fun that the reader gets to put the pieces together. +1

However, it is kind of true that the suicide note should make the mechanics of the scip obvious to the Foundation. Foundation personnel outside of the Site would still have access to this document, and be able to put 2 and 2 together.

This could be remedied by the addition of correspondence between some off-site Admins or researchers. Wouldn't have to be explicit, just an indication that they at least know what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or it was implied that someone did start to figure it out, but got ganked by the knife?

That was actually the idea behind the occurrence involving a researcher getting killed. The death of the researcher led to knowledge of the researcher being 'erased', which is why the Foundation couldn't figure out what was going on until two tests later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a cool concept executed well, but despite your goal, I simply don't find it credible that it never occurred to the Foundation what was happening when it's immediately obvious to the reader. And the note guy, for that matter.

Reluctantly downvoted.

Maybe if there was some suggestion that there's an effect preventing anyone but the dreamers from realizing what's happening? Or it was implied that someone did start to figure it out, but got ganked by the knife?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see a reason why the smell might be detectible where the rest isn't; the same reason the guy has a crib in his big empty house; physical objects from these things seem to hang around even as memories and perception are removed. And smell is physical objects, albeit very small ones. The item's effect just prevents anyone putting two and two together.

Quite apart from that an article with some great creeping dread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a thing what makes you go crazy… but it's a good one! I really like perception screws and this is executed with the right amount of pacing and colour, I would say. Perhaps I haven't read enough of the other articles with similar themes. I think this would be my first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a picture to the article. Source is from wiki commons, available here; I've also added the _image tag so it can be verified as CC compliant!

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: Same.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I found this delightfully creepy, and I didn't realize it was a HippoSkip until I was done recording it. My upvote is gotten!

https://youtu.be/7C90SIkPe8k

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of unknowingly killing someone and erasing them from everyone's memory is spine-chilling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit I figured out what was happening after the first test, but specifically because I myself wrote It's Just A Dream, as mentioned above. Still, I feel this was quite well executed.

(It's Just A Dream, incidentally, was my first ever article for the site.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I wrote this before I read some other SCP-stuff that included the idea already (like the highly excellent There Is No Antimemetics Division); while I thought it was pretty terrifying and novel at the time, I now realize it's actually something that's happened a lot in Foundation lore.

ETA: That is to say, I'm still pretty happy with this, but I completely understand why someone would fail to find it even slightly intriguing given that the Foundation-verse is already filled with memetically cloaked corpses. The only difference here is that these ones still stink.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This (there are dead people but the Foundation can't see them) is unfortunately one of those things which is only a trope inside the Foundation-verse, but it's been done enough times here that something more needs to be done with it for it to hold my attention. It's competent, but it needs more to distinguish it from the crowd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Killed it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't know whether this was good or bad, but I saw it coming miles away. In fact, while starting to read I thought "huh, there's a good chance this will eventually notify me about the disappereance of some D-class or something like that", and presto! it was like that.

Anyways, it was kind of easy to see through the lore, but it didn't stop to be chilling and enjoyable to read, so I'll arbitrarily decide it was a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Hey, nothing wrong with horror fluff! I'm not against the smell, just pointing out the overall pattern.

It also apparently made enough sense to a lot of people. Point being, IMO, it doesn't need an ax to it. Tweaking that paragraph/list would help, but it's your prerogative.

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: I really really appreciate the Foundation being fallible but not stupid in this. One of my favorite themes to explore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the response!

+ show block
– hide block

I see what you mean re: the imprecision of "rust-red"; I think "rust-colored" would work much better (as it tells us much more — it's not rusty, it just has the *coloration* of rust). I'm not sure if there's a more technical term for spoiled flesh, though; I'll have to think. Also, I see what you mean re: curiosities — I'll change that!

Re: Erasing of history… this is something I think I've failed to imply strongly enough throughout the story, as multiple people have brought this up (even in this very thread!). The knife doesn't alter reality in any way — it has no retrocausal component whatsoever — it only has an antimemetic component. It merely renders its victims incapable of being perceived… physically, mentally, and even conceptually.

It isn't that documentation is changed; it's that you literally can't see their names on the document anymore — if you had a piece of paper with all the names of the victims on it, when you look at it, you'd just see a blank piece of paper (you might not even see the paper, depending!). Bureaucracies don't get restructured to account for their disappearance; the gaps are still there, but the bureaucracies can't perceive those gaps because the antimemetic effect is so powerful it renders the gaps themselves invisible. They're just left with these… bizarre, confusing discrepancies ("Why didn't we send enough D-Class personnel to this site?"). That, plus the fact that people's brains fill these perception-gaps in different ways, is what clued the Foundation off to something fishy going on.

As for the smell, you're right; there's absolutely no justification for that — that's pure horror fluff on my part. >_> I knew it when I included it. The antimemetic effect should extend to the smell, too, but it doesn't because I wanted some way of implying that the bodies were still there (plus, in my head-cannon, the mythical entity responsible for this is associated with rotting flesh, so… it's basically just dream-logic >_>)

The fact that none of this is getting across is… well, it's a weakness on the article's part, and something I'm going to try to address, I think!

I appreciate this — you've given me some stuff to think about. I don't think the article is getting across what I want to get it across (that the red knife does not, in any way, alter reality; it just has an absurdly powerful antimemetic effect). I'm going to have to think about how to fix that.

EDIT:

+ The Videos
– hide block

To help illustrate what I'm trying to get across — and maybe this is the bit I should focus on — if you sat down and watched the video of the test, you'd probably think it was ten minutes long. Anyone watching you watch the video would notice much more time passing by, though. Like, that's the power of this effect; you don't even notice there are segments of the video you can't perceive — you just stare blankly at the screen, not noticing that minutes, or even hours are passing.

The Foundation only picked up on this particular effect when somebody happened to notice that the files listed the videos as much longer than what the people who sat down and watched them claimed they were.

I think I failed to get this effect across >_> Maybe, instead of the memo, something describing the discrepancy of the video itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stuff! (Oh, and I didn't take it as confrontational, and I'm not intending to argue either.)

+ show block
– hide block

To the first bit, specifically:

The knife's surface is rust-red and smells of spoiled flesh.

This isn't a particularly useful technical description. Is it actually rusted/rust? Is 'spoiled flesh' a technical term, and is there a better term for that smell?

Daniel Crenshall, an elderly recluse and collector of numerous 'curiosities' who committed suicide on 02-15-1987.

Does 'curiosities' include any other anomalies, or just antiques/oddities/curios/whatever? Leaving it vague isn't great procedure.

To the second part:
- It's possible to derive some motivation for an antimemetic killer behind this stuff, whether it be the knife or some force or a monster, but yeah, it isn't very present. It's still kind of a weak story element, which just means the antimemetic story shoulders the burden.

- To the antimemetic story, the initial mistakes are credible, and the buildup as he starts to realize the problems is neat. The problem is one of internal consistency/logic, and really comes in the second to last collapsible. This thing is powerful enough to erase people from all Foundation records… except for a few inconsistencies. So why these inconsistencies? In Introductory Antimemetics, there's no physical and very little digital evidence of the victims: we only see any of it because the narrator is the target. In SCP-2454, there's only one remnant, and the Foundation is otherwise barely catching on.

Back to this one, if their corpses are present but imperceptible, why just the smell? Why are video records a cognitohazard while all other records including world-spanning digital networks have been blanked or modified? Does it re-erase/edit history/information with every kill? It's basically gone from a nearly omnipotent effect (erasing direct perception, electronic records, memory, etc.) to what must either be a complex of undefined anomalous powers, or an omnipotent ability with exceedingly arbitrary limitations. I'm all for limited powers and the Foundation recognizing a mistake, but the lapses just don't make much sense, and strong logic's kind of necessary when you're playing with missing knowledge. That, or thematic dream-logic (at the other extreme), and this didn't read as that either.

I'm not saying that's the worst thing on earth, mind. I didn't downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obviously, you're under no obligation to respond, but — if I might ask — what parts of the description were colloquial/poetic? My initial draft had a lot of that in it; I went through numerous revisions trying to excise all 'purple-y' elements and make it as technically concise as possible. I'm mostly asking because — if I missed a few spots — I want to know about them, so I can fix them (or at least avoid making the same mistake in other articles!).

Re: The other bits — I know the article needs to stand alone; if it didn't work on its own, I can't make it work by 'explaining' certain elements of it after the fact. That being said, because I'm a completionist:
+ show block
– hide block
  • The cannibalism/heart thing is supposed to be part of a greater mythology in my head (involving an anomalous entity that knows that which no one else knows, and hence seeks to destroy knowledge — specifically, compelling people to kill and devour each other with the knife, so no one will remember them, and they therefore become knowledge the entity possesses), but as that isn't really clarified here, I see what you mean about it just being a knife that drives you to eat people (and then forget that you ate people). I think that was my biggest fear with the article — that ultimately, it comes off as just a creepy knife that drives you to kill and devour hearts.
  • I was hoping to make the Foundation's mistake here credible — specifically, I was hoping that the only reason we realize something is wrong ourselves is because the article tells you that something is wrong at the outset. It's my hope, then, that a reasonably intelligent person reading this article without that warning would start to realize something is wrong around the same time the tester realized something was wrong (by the second to last test, culminating in the last test — when he realized he was suffering from some sort of antimemetic effect and immediately ceased testing). The scent could be initially explained by the knife (I hoped), and the failure to include more than one D-Class personnel was (again, I hoped) merely some minor incompetence/miscommunication. I guess my point here is that making sure the mistake was credible was important to me; if I failed to accomplish that, then I need to invest more effort into making these mistakes more credible in the future!

Thank you for the feedback! I appreciate it!

(Also, I hope none of the above comes off as confrontational, or me trying to argue with you. >_>)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-voting.

For what this is, this is done fairly well. The idea of D-Class being sent in en mass and the suicide note were neat.

Unfortunately, "The Foundation doesn't notice the bodies piling up" is a actually pretty common story on the wiki. This isn't as in-universe consistent/clever as, say, SCP-2454. We as readers get to see almost everything that they're overlooking, and it kind of strains credulity that it blocks them in these specific, set ways with nobody noticing.

The fact that it's in service of a gross red knife that makes you cut out and eat hearts really doesn't help anything. That's textbook 'spooky', and the imagery (heart-eating, cannibalism) doesn't feel worked in or tied to anything in particular, so it's just there. Also, the description you gave the knife and some other portions of the description are colloquial/poetic rather than clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my head canon this is how most SCPs go before the researchers figure out what's going on. The only thing out of place is that we're in the 3000 series now, whereas this would have fit better earlier in the foundation's evolution. But that can hardly be held against it.

I guess slowing down "the reveal" is that the people who would figure it out are actually disappearing or already affected. At the same time, there's no keter danger apparent so the higher-ups don't care too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well implemented and horrible in a good way.
The Foundation are little dim in not realising what's happening (you'd think they'd be up on there antimemetic risks and thing-that-erases-people by now). But as i said well executed so who cares :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was after the third test log that I got an idea about what was going on and after the fourth I was kinda sure I had it but like the others it was that note at the end that really hit it home for me in what this thing was doing. +1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always a sucker for retroactive anomalies, and this is as well-executed as any of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, fuck you for writing this.

+1 with the power of a thousand suns. Damn, that's creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, you already know what I think of it. :) Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done! I liked the logs, although my initial interpretation was that this was some kind of retrocausal thing. +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really well done, the reveal is nice and organic and hits pretty hard. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see this on the site! Enjoyed it on irc, enjoy it now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The notes sealed it for me as well. Interesting, intriguing and creepy enough without overstaying its welcome. It peers into the humanity of the researchers, something they probably forget themselves as well.

The only real nagging thought I have is that I don't think we can actually recognize faces in dreams. But due to the nature of dreams itself, oneirology is an inexact science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the feedback, as well as the link! I was not familiar with that story, but I see what you mean; the test logs are very reminiscent!

Regarding a rewrite with focus more on the note:
+ show block
– hide block

I hope the following doesn't come off as defensive — as I accept that you might be right! — but I want to wait a little bit and see how the article fares before making any decisions about heavy rewrites.

I gave the note less focus because I felt this would maximize its impact; by the time you reach it, you've probably forgotten the circumstances under which SCP-3041 was discovered, and you've been reading a fairly clinical (albeit grotesque) account wherein SCP-3041's actual properties are revealed… the note is meant to sucker-punch you out of no where by taking that new knowledge and making it suddenly, deeply personal. In other words, I'm skeptical whether or not the note would have the same impact without the test logs and memo preceding it!

I also don't think I could expand this into a tale; I just don't feel like there's enough for me to tease out of it (though someone else more than welcome to try!). If it can't stand on its own as an SCP (and I'm willing to accept that it might not), I'll just dump it and offer the concept up to anyone else who wants to take a stab at it (no pun intended).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so I was going to downvote because this was reminding me too much of WrongJohnSilver's It's Just A Dream, but that last note was chilling enough to save this. Novote.

E: If I were you, I'd focus on this article's strong suit—the note, and the feeling of dread and despair that it evoked. I, personally, think that it might be a good idea to try and make this a tale, maybe depicting the man trying to figure out whether he's going crazy or whether something darker is occurring.

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
All the lonely people
Where do they all come from?
All the lonely people
Where do they all belong?

SOURCES:

  • Image from here.
  • Inspired by Stephen King's short story, Strawberry Spring.

FEEDBACK:

  • jishtrans
  • Photosynthetic (SCP-628 - Flute Corpse).
AUTHOR NOTES
– hide block

The knife compels you to kill and eat your victims' hearts. Victims suffer an antimemetic affect: They can no longer be seen. This effect extends to their names, memories, documents, videos, and even their flesh and bone. No one can directly perceive them, but they're still there (hence the smell of rotting meat).

All that remains are your dreams of the act.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
